summary.narrative: Across his current term, Johnson advanced several of his stated 2022 priorities: a violent-crime reduction plan tied to documented declines in violent crime, a convention-center redevelopment funded by a 2022 voter proposition, and property tax rate reductions (he pushed for deeper cuts than the council adopted and voted against budgets he viewed as too high). Housing and Hensley Field plans exist but show partial or stalled implementation, the latter blocked by federal cleanup litigation, while the climate action plan shows only preliminary staff-level steps. The pattern reflects consistent advocacy on his core public-safety and fiscal priorities, with delivery constrained by Dallas's weak-mayor structure and a council Johnson does not control.
summary.grade_rationale: Most tracked promises reached intermediate-to-advanced delivery (D3-D4) with documented action and citywide scale, but the mayor's causal role is consistently moderate (0.4-0.6) given the council-manager system, and lower-priority commitments like the climate plan stalled at the commitment/preliminary stage. The mix of genuine advancement on core priorities against limited or externally-blocked progress elsewhere drives a middling record.

promises.text: Implement the violent crime reduction plan and provide the Dallas Police Department with best training, modernized equipment, technology, and facilities.
what_happened: 2018-2019: Johnson formed a task force to identify evidence-based non-policing crime strategies (c115).
Dec 1, 2023: Johnson praised DPD for yearly declines in violent crime totals in 2021 and 2022 (c117).
Jul 17, 2024: Johnson highlighted the city's decrease in violent crime (c109).
Nov 13, 2025: State of the City touted lower violent crime (c125).

promises.text: Reduce the property tax rate to make Dallas more competitive with surrounding cities.
what_happened: Sep 22-24, 2023: Council passed budget with a smaller property tax rate reduction; Johnson voted against it, calling the cut insufficient (c118, c111).
Fall 2024: Council approved Dallas's largest rate cut in at least 40 years (c120).
Sep 15, 2025: Johnson urged colleagues to submit budget amendments reducing spending to allow a reduced tax rate (c95).

promises.text: Develop and recommend a plan for the future of the Kay Bailey Hutchison Convention Center and surrounding area.
what_happened: Sep 15, 2022: Johnson held a news conference urging voters to approve the proposition to help pay for a new convention center (c37).
Nov 2022: Voters approved Prop A, funding KBHCC and Fair Park renovations via a 2% hotel occupancy tax increase (c26).
2026: KBHCC redevelopment cited as a major investment spurring growth (c127).

promises.text: Create a master plan for Hensley Field.
what_happened: Aug 21, 2023: Master plan for Hensley Field redevelopment exists but implementation awaits resolution of Dallas-U.S. Navy cleanup litigation (c131).

promises.text: Implement the Dallas Real-Time Rapid Rehousing Strategy and provide recommendations on affordable housing.
what_happened: Feb 2023: Johnson formed a task force on the city's homelessness response (c132).
Mar 8, 2023: 1,700+ people reported moved into permanent residence since the rapid rehousing program launched in 2021 (c133).
2024: Homeless population reported decreased only slightly (~160) from 2022 (c35).

promises.text: Implement the Comprehensive Environmental and Climate Action Plan.
what_happened: Jul 24, 2024: City Office of Environmental Quality compiled a greenhouse gas emissions inventory; commentary urged Johnson to lead on climate (c137).
Dec 2024: Johnson's stated agenda centered on public safety, parks, potholes, property taxes — no climate plan advancement cited (c143).

data_gaps: Dallas has a council-manager (weak-mayor) system (c89), materially limiting the mayor's direct authority — reflected in conservative their_role anchors. Almost all promise candidates are Tier-3; only a Tier-1 source is required downstream before any D3/D4 delivery score is final, so these delivery buckets should be treated as AI drafts pending Tier-1 corroboration. Several candidates (party switch, recall, 'not running' statements, generic 100-day-plan boilerplate, and unrelated Eric Johnsons) were excluded as non-promises. Property-tax and rehousing classifications are CONTESTED given documented shortfalls versus claimed success.

provisional_status.display: Grade is record-to-date — term in progress, 75.1% elapsed (as of 2026-06-22). It will change as the term continues.
provisional_status.note: Distinct from any per-promise 'PROVISIONAL (early-term)' flag in scorecard.card_flags, which concerns a single promise's own deadline, not the term's completeness.
